title: Blown cellulose insulation for cavity walls
subtitle: How recycled paper fibre compares with mineral wool bead as a cavity fill, and what to check before installing
excerpt: Cellulose fibre (shredded and treated recycled newspaper) is blown into masonry cavity walls as an alternative to mineral wool beads or EPS beads. It performs similarly at lower embodied carbon and is hygroscopic, which can help manage occasional moisture in the cavity. The installation process requires CIGA-registered installers and a survey to confirm cavity condition. This guide covers the comparison with mineral wool bead, the pre-installation checks, and the post-installation signs of a good installation.
sourceNotes: Cavity Insulation Guarantee Agency (CIGA) technical standards. BRE Report BR 262 on cavity wall insulation. NSAI technical guidance on blown cellulose insulation. BS 8208:2015 on assessment of suitability of external cavity walls for filling. gov.uk ECO4 scheme guidance on eligible insulation products.

glossaryTerms.definition: A wall built in two separate leaves, typically 102.5 mm brick outer and 100 mm block inner, with a 50 to 100 mm air gap between them. The cavity reduces rain penetration and can be filled with insulation to improve thermal performance.
A measure of how much heat flows through one square metre of a building element per degree of temperature difference. Measured in W/m²·K.

content.text: of around 1.4 W/m²·K. Filling the cavity with blown mineral wool or cellulose drops this to 0.45 to 0.55 W/m²·K, reducing wall heat loss by about 65 percent. Blown cellulose and blown mineral wool bead deliver similar U-values in a 50 mm cavity. The differences lie in embodied carbon and hygroscopic behaviour.
Cellulose vs mineral wool bead: the comparison
Thermal performance: similar. Both achieve around 0.038 to 0.040 W/m·K lambda value when settled in a 50 mm cavity.
Embodied carbon: cellulose is significantly lower. It is made from recycled newspaper with borate treatment. Mineral wool requires energy-intensive glass or rock melting.

Hygroscopic behaviour: cellulose absorbs and releases moisture without losing its insulating value at low moisture levels. If the cavity gets damp occasionally, cellulose copes better than glass bead. Wet glass bead can compact and sink, leaving voids.
Installers: both products need a CIGA-registered installer. Cellulose specialists are rarer than mineral wool bead installers. In rural areas, expect a longer wait and higher quotes.

A CIGA installer must survey the wall to BS 8208 before drilling. They check: cavity width (50 mm minimum, no rubble), outer leaf condition (no failed mortar or cracked bricks), DPC line, window reveals, and chimney zones where the cavity narrows.

On a cold day 6 to 12 months after installation, thermal imaging of the inner wall face should show a uniform temperature with no cold spots. Cold spots indicate voids where fill did not reach. All drill holes in the outer leaf should be repointed to match the original mortar colour and profile. The CIGA certificate guarantees the installation for 25 years; keep it with the property documents.

attrs.symptom: Damp patches appearing on the inner leaf after installation

content.text: This is the most serious post-installation problem with any cavity fill. Stop and contact the installer immediately. They must investigate whether the fill has bridged the cavity and conducted water from the outer to the inner leaf. A CIGA claim covers remediation if the installation was non-compliant.
